1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
275	    \;\cong\;
276	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
277	  \]
278	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
279	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
280	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
281	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
282	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
283	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
284	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
285	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
286	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
287	\end{lemma}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
291	  \leanok
292	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
293	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
294	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
295	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
296	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
297	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
298	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
299	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
300	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
301	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
302	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
303	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
304	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
305	
306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
308	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
310	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
311	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto
313	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
314	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
315	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
316	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
317	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
318	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
319	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
320	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
321	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
322	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
323	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
324	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
325	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
327	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
328	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
329	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
330	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
331	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
332	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
333	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
334	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	
340	\leanok
341	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
342	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
344	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
345	  restriction map
346	  \[
347	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
348	  \]
349	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
350	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
351	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
352	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
353	\end{lemma}
354	
355	\begin{proof}
356	
357	\leanok
358	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
359	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
360	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
361	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
362	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
363	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
364	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
365	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
366	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
367	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
368	\end{proof}
369	
370	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
371	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
372	on their proof sketches.
373	
374	\begin{lemma}
375	
376	\leanok
377	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
378	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
380	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
381	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
382	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
383	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
384	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
385	  itself.
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	
390	
391	\leanok
392	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
393	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
394	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
395	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
396	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
398	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
399	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
400	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
401	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
402	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
403	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
404	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
405	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
406	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
407	  basic-open computation below.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{lemma}
411	
412	\leanok
413	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
414	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
416	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
417	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
418	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
419	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
420	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
421	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
422	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	
427	
428	\leanok
429	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
430	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
431	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
432	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
433	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
434	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
435	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
436	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
437	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
438	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
439	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
446	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
448	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
449	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
450	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
451	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
452	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
453	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
454	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
455	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
456	  \[
457	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
458	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
459	  \]
460	\end{lemma}
461	
462	\begin{proof}
463	  \leanok
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
465	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
466	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
467	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
468	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
469	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
471	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
472	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
473	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
474	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
475	\end{proof}
476	
477	\begin{lemma}\leanok
478	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
479	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
481	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
482	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
483	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
484	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
485	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
486	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
487	% is hand-proved per a.
488	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
489	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
490	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
491	  \[
492	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
493	    \;\cong\;
494	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
495	  \]
496	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
497	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
498	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
499	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
500	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
501	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
502	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
503	
504	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
505	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
506	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
507	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
508	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
509	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
510	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
511	\end{lemma}
512	
513	\begin{proof}
514	  \leanok
515	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
516	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
517	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
518	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
519	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
520	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
521	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
522	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
523	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
524	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
525	
526	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
527	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
528	  \[
529	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
530	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
531	  \]
532	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
533	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
534	
535	  \medskip
536	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
537	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
538	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
539	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
540	
541	  \medskip
542	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
543	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
544	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
545	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
546	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
547	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
548	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
550	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
551	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
552	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
553	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
554	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
555	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
556	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
557	  an isomorphism.
558	
559	  \medskip
560	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
561	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
562	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
563	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
564	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
565	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
566	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
567	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
568	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
569	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
570	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
571	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
572	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
573	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
574	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
575	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
576	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
577	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
578	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
580	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
581	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
582	  movements.
583	
584	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
585	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
586	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
587	  \[
588	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
589	      \;\cong\;
590	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
591	  \]
592	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
593	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
594	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
595	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
596	
597	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
598	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
599	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
601	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
602	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
603	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
604	  \[
605	    \begin{array}{ccc}
606	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
607	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
608	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
609	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
610	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
611	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
612	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
613	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
614	    \end{array}
615	  \]
616	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
617	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
618	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
619	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
620	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
621	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
622	  \(\widetilde M\).
623	
624	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
625	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
626	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
627	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
628	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
629	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
630	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
631	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
632	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
633	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
634	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
635	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
636	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
637	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
638	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
639	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
640	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
641	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
642	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
643	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
644	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
645	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
646	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
647	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
648	
649	  \medskip
650	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
651	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
652	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
653	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
654	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
655	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
656	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
657	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
658	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
659	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
660	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
661	  naturality square for the inclusion
662	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
663	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
664	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
665	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
666	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
667	
668	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
669	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
670	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
671	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
672	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
673	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
674	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
675	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
676	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
677	
678	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
679	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
680	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
681	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
682	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
683	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
684	  % bump.
685	  \medskip
686	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
687	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
688	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
689	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
690	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
691	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
692	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
693	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
694	  needed for the affine lane.
695	\end{proof}
696	
697	\begin{remark}
698	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
699	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
700	  \begin{enumerate}
701	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
702	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
703	      \(R\)-module of sections of
704	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
705	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
706	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
707	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
708	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
709	      closed under isomorphism; hence
710	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
711	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
712	      needed for the affine lane.
713	  \end{enumerate}
714	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
715	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
716	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
717	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
718	\end{remark}

720	\subsection{The pullback companion}
721	
722	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
723	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
724	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
725	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
726	
727	\begin{lemma}
728	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
729	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
730	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
731	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
732	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
733	  % L1241--1256).
734	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
735	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
736	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
737	  % be affine schemes.
738	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
739	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
740	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
741	  % \begin{enumerate}
742	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
743	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
744	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
745	  % in the $S$-module $N$.
746	  % \end{enumerate}"
747	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
748	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
749	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
750	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
751	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
752	  \[
753	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
754	    \;\cong\;
755	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
756	  \]
757	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
758	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
759	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
760	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
761	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
762	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
763	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
764	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
765	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)
766	  of the same Stacks lemma of which the pushforward formula is part~(2).
767	\end{lemma}
768	
769	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
770	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
771	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."
772	\begin{proof}
773	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
774	  Pullback along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, on quasi-coherent sheaves, the
775	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
776	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
777	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
778	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
779	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
780	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
781	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
782	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
783	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
784	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
785	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
786	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong
787	  \widetilde{(R' \otimes_R M)}\). Naturality in \(M\) is the naturality of that
788	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on
789	  \(D(\varphi a)\) the localization \((R' \otimes_R M)[1/a]\) is
790	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
791	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)
792	\end{proof}

852	% plumbing. The single Mathlib-absent ingredient is

858	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
859	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
860	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
861	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
862	  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
863	  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
864	  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
865	  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
866	  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
867	  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
868	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
869	  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
870	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
871	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
872	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
873	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
874	
875	  \medskip
876	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
877	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
878	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
879	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
880	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
881	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
882	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
883	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
884	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
885	  \[
886	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
887	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
888	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
889	  \]
890	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
891	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
892	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
893	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
894	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
895	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
896	  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
897	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
898	  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
899	  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
900	  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.
901	
902	  \medskip
903	  \emph{Identification of the two sides via the affine dictionaries.} The two
904	  quasi-coherent sheaves whose comparison \(\alpha\) is the section-level
905	  base-change map are identified over the affine chart by the two affine
906	  dictionaries. On the one hand, the \emph{pullback dictionary}
907	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} identifies the restriction of
908	  \((g')^*\mathcal{F} = (g')^*(\widetilde{M})\) to the chart with the tilde of the
909	  base-changed module: writing \(g' = \operatorname{Spec}(\theta)\) for the induced
910	  ring map \(\theta : A \to R' \otimes_R A\),
911	  \[
912	    (g')^*\widetilde{M} \;\cong\; \widetilde{\bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr)},
913	  \]
914	  the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\);
915	  pushing forward along the affine \(f' = \operatorname{Spec}(R \to R' \otimes_R A)\)
916	  and then reading global sections recovers \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an
917	  \(R'\)-module by restriction of scalars
918	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}). On the other hand, the
919	  \emph{pushforward dictionary} Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
920	  identifies \(f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*(\widetilde{M})\) over \(S\) with
921	  \(\widetilde{\operatorname{restr}_{R \to A} M}\), and the pullback dictionary
922	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} then identifies its pullback
923	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F})\) with \(\widetilde{R' \otimes_R M}\); reading global
924	  sections gives the \(R'\)-module \(R' \otimes_R M\). Thus, under the dictionaries,
925	  the source and target of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) are the two iterated tensor products
926	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and \(R' \otimes_R M\), both viewed as
927	  \(R'\)-modules.
928	
929	  \medskip
930	  \emph{Identification of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) with the cancellation isomorphism.} The
931	  base-change map \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is the global-sections incarnation of
932	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}),
933	  which is constructed abstractly as an \emph{adjoint mate}: it is the transpose,
934	  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunctions for \(g\) and
935	  \(g'\), of the morphism obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
936	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The crux of the remaining Lean work is
937	  to match this abstractly-defined adjoint mate, transported through the two
938	  dictionaries above, with the \emph{concrete} canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison
939	  between the two iterated tensor products — that is, with the associativity /
940	  cancellation isomorphism
941	  \[
942	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
943	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M
944	  \]
945	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
946	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). Concretely the map
947	  sends \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\), using the
948	  \(A\)-action on \(M\). Once the identification of the abstract adjoint mate with
949	  this concrete \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is made — the sole nontrivial
950	  remaining obligation — the affine case closes immediately, because
951	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis
952	  whatsoever; flatness of \(g\) enters only in the global (non-affine) statement
953	  via the {\v C}ech argument, not here.
954	\end{proof}

1082	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and

1084	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1085	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module

1087	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1088	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
1089	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
1090	  \(B\)-modules

1092	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1093	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1094	  \]
1095	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.

1097	  \medskip
1098	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
1099	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
1100	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
1101	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
1102	  computes sheaf cohomology:
1103	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
1104	  the cover yields an affine open cover
1105	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
1106	  \(X_B\), with the same property
1107	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
1108	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
1109	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
1110	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
1111	  \[
1112	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
1113	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
1114	  \]
1115	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
1116	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
1117	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
1118	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).

1120	  \medskip

1124	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
1125	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
1126	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
1127	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
1128	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
1129	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
1130	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
1131	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
1132	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
1133	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through

1135	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
1136	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
1137	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of

1048	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
1049	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
1050	
1051	\emph{Full-monoidal-category construction --- off path, not to be formalized.} The
1052	construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on all of
1053	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1054	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1055	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1056	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1057	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1058	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1059	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1060	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator is obtained as the sheafification
1061	transport of the presheaf associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), whose
1062	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation is closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
1063	commutation (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,%
1064	lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); it uses \emph{no} \emph{full}
1065	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1066	monoidal category. Accordingly the \emph{full monoidal-category} target of this
1067	section --- the \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W})
1068	and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) --- is off
1069	path and must not be re-attempted; it is kept for the historical record. The d.1/d.2
1070	stalk apparatus it shares with the live single whisker obligation is \emph{not}
1071	vestigial, however: it is on the critical path through
1072	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1073	
1074	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1075	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1076	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1077	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1078	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1079	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1080	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1081	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1082	
1083	\paragraph{What the group law consumes.} The group law is assembled directly on
1084	isomorphism classes of \emph{locally trivial} (invertible) sheaves
1085	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard
1086	group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1087	(\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). It needs only the \emph{existence} of
1088	the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1089	and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, together with \(\otimes\)-invertibility
1090	supplying inverses; it uses \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance, \emph{no}
1091	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\).
1092	It does, however, consume the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, which is
1093	realized as the sheafification transport of the presheaf associator and whose
1094	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1095	is closed by the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}).
1096	Hence the stalk ingredients (d.1)/(d.2) are \emph{on} the critical path of the group
1097	law --- through the associator's single whisker obligation --- even though the full
1098	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of
1099	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) (the subject of the present section) is \emph{not} built:
1100	that full monoidal generality, with its \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields
1101	(\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) and all derived coherence, is what the group law does not
1102	consume. The single open obligation of the present section's full-monoidal target is
1103	thus the local-injectivity half packaged for an arbitrary whisker object
1104	(\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), itself a consequence of the same d.2
1105	commutation.

1107	The following lemma is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk-map packaging (the ``d.1''
1108	ingredient). It is consumed by the stalk--tensor commutation of
1109	\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation,
1110	lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}) and is therefore a live ingredient
1111	of the unconditional associator route.

1113	\begin{lemma}
1114	% Live ingredient: consumed by lem:stalk_tensor_commutation and
1115	% lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk (sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor).
1116	
1117	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1118	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1119	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1120	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1121	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1122	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1123	  \[
1124	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1125	  \]
1126	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1127	  characterised on germs by
1128	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1129	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1130	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1131	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1132	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1133	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1134	
1135	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1136	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1137	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of
1138	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} and
1139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. They build on the
1140	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1141	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1142	\end{lemma}
1143	
1144	\begin{proof}
1145	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1146	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1147	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1148	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1149	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1150	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1151	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1152	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1153	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1154	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1155	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1156	\end{proof}

1161	to the associator. That \emph{full monoidal category} is not built: the group law on
1162	iso-classes consumes only the \emph{existence} of the associator
1163	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The associator instead arises as the
1164	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
1165	live lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. The stalk ingredients
1166	(d.1)/(d.2) themselves --- the \(R_x\)-linear stalk map \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}

1168	\emph{on} the critical path through that live lemma; what is off path is only the
1169	\emph{full-generality} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging of the following
1170	duplicate, retained for the historical record.

1172	\begin{lemma}
1173	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.

1176	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1177	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1178	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1179	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1180	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1181	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1182	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1183	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1184	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1185	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1186	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1187	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1188	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1189	  left-whiskered morphism
1190	  \[
1191	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1192	  \]
1193	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1194	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1195	
1196	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1197	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1198	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1199	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1200	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1201	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1202	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1203	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1204	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1205	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1206	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1207	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1208	\end{lemma}
1209	
1210	\begin{proof}
1211	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1212	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1213	
1214	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1215	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1216	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1217	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1218	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1219	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1220	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1221	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1222	  On such a \(V\):
1223	  \begin{itemize}
1224	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1225	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1226	      \[
1227	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1228	      \]
1229	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1230	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1231	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1232	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1233	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1234	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1235	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1236	  \end{itemize}
1237	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1238	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1239	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1240	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1241	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1242	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1243	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1244	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1245	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1246	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1247	
1248	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1249	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1250	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1251	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1252	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1253	
1254	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1255	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1256	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1257	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1258	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1259	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1260	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1261	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1262	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1263	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1264	  \[
1265	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1266	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1267	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1268	  \]
1269	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1270	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1271	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1272	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1273	
1274	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1275	  pinned commit:
1276	  \begin{enumerate}
1277	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1278	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1279	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1280	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1281	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1282	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1283	      above.
1284	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1285	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1286	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1287	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1288	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1289	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1290	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1291	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1292	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1293	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1294	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1295	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1296	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1297	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1298	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1299	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1300	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1301	  \end{enumerate}
1302	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1303	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1304	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1305	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1306	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1307	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1308	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1309	
1310	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1311	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1312	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1313	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1314	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1315	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1316	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1317	\end{proof}

1327	\begin{lemma}
1328	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1329	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1330	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1331	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1332	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1333	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1334	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1335	  \(J.W\):
1336	  \[
1337	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1338	    \qquad
1339	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1340	  \]
1341	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1342	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1343	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1344	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1345	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1346	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1347	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1348	\end{lemma}
1349	
1350	\begin{proof}
1351	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1352	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1353	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1354	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1355	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1356	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1357	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1358	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1359	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1360	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1361	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1362	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1363	\end{proof}

1377	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1378	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1380	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1381	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1382	  instance
1383	  \[
1384	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1385	  \]
1386	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1387	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1388	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1389	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1390	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1391	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1392	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1393	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1394	  category
1395	  \[
1396	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1397	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1398	  \]
1399	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1400	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1401	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1402	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1403	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1404	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1405	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1406	  composites.
1407	
1408	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1409	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1410	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1411	\end{lemma}

1413	\begin{proof}
1414	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1415	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1416	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1417	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1418	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1419	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1420	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1421	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1422	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1423	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1425	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1426	  at any point.
1427	\end{proof}

1429	\begin{lemma}
1430	
1431

1433	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ (unconditional)]
1434	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1435	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1436	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1437	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1438	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1439	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1440	  \[
1441	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1444	  \]
1445	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1446	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
