1764	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1765	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1766	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1767	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1768	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1769	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1770	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1771	\end{lemma}
1772	
1773	\begin{proof}
1774	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1775	  Closure is exactly
1776	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1777	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1778	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1779	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1780	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1781	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1782	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1783	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1784	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1785	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1786	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1787	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1788	\end{proof}
1789	
1790	\begin{lemma}
1791	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1792	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1794	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1795	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1796	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1797	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1798	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1799	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1800	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1801	  functor
1802	  \[
1803	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1804	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1805	  \]
1806	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1807	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1808	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1809	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1810	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1811	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1812	  canonical isomorphism
1813	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1814	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1815	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1816	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1817	\end{lemma}
1818	
1819	\begin{proof}
1820	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1821	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1822	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1823	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1824	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1825	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1826	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1827	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1828	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1829	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1830	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1831	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1832	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1833	  same cover.
1834	\end{proof}

1836	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1837	left-whiskering}
1838	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1839	
1840	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1841	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1842	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1843	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1844	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1845	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1846	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1847	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1848	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1849	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1850	
1851	\begin{lemma}
1852	
1853	\leanok
1854	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1855	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1856	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1857	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1858	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1859	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1860	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1861	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1862	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1863	  % "\begin{lemma}
1864	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1865	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1866	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1867	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1868	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1869	  % $$
1870	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1871	  % =
1872	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1873	  % $$
1874	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1875	  % \end{lemma}"
1876	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1877	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1878	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1879	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1880	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1881	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1882	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1883	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1884	  \[
1885	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1886	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1887	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1888	  \]
1889	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1890	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1891	\end{lemma}
1892	
1893	\begin{proof}
1894	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1895	  \leanok
1896	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1897	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1898	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1899	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1900	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1901	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1902	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1903	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1904	  inversion.
1905	
1906	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1907	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1908	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1909	  \[
1910	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1911	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1912	  \]
1913	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1914	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1915	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1916	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1917	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1918	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1919	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1920	  \[
1921	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1922	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1923	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1924	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1925	  \]
1926	
1927	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1928	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1929	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1930	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1931	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1932	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1933	  forward additive comparison map
1934	  \[
1935	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1936	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1937	  \]
1938	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1939	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1940	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1941	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1942	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1943	
1944	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1945	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1946	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1947	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1948	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1949	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1950	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1951	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1952	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1953	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1954	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1955	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1956	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1957	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1958	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1959	
1960	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1961	  \[
1962	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1963	  \]
1964	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1965	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1966	  \[
1967	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1968	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1969	  \]
1970	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1971	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1972	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1973	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1974	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1975	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1976	  neighbourhood system.
1977	
1978	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1979	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1980	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1981	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1982	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1983	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1984	  \[
1985	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1986	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1987	  \]
1988	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
1989	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
1990	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
1991	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
1992	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
1993	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
1994	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
1995	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
1996	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
1997	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
1998	
1999	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2000	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
2001	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
2002	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
2003	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
2004	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
2005	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2006	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2007	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2008	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2009	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2010	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
Let me continue reading more of this section to find the `sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality` section.

2011	
2012	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
2013	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
2014	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
2015	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
2016	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
2017	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
2018	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
2019	  it.
2020	
2021	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
2022	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
2023	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
2024	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
2025	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
2026	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
2027	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
2028	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
2029	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
2030	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
2031	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
2032	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2033	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2034	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2035	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2036	
2037	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2038	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2039	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2040	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2041	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2042	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2043	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2044	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2045	  inverse.
2046	\end{proof}
2047	
2048	\begin{lemma}
2049	
2050	\leanok
2051	[Forward stalk--tensor comparison map (d.2, partial)]
2052	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}
2053	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc}
2054	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2055	  With \(R\), \(A\), \(B\), \(x\) as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, there is
2056	  a canonical natural \emph{additive} comparison map
2057	  \[
2058	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
2059	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\;
2060	      A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
2061	  \]
2062	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
2063	  \[
2064	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr)
2065	      \;=\; \mathrm{germ}_x a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x b .
2066	  \]
2067	  This is the \emph{forward half} of the isomorphism
2068	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}: the additive comparison map of
2069	  stages (i)--(ii) of that lemma's proof, prior to the \(R_x\)-linearity upgrade
2070	  (iii) and the construction of the reverse map (iv).
2071	\end{lemma}
2072	
2073	\begin{lemma}
2074	
2075	\leanok
2076	[\(R_x\)-linear stalk--tensor comparison map (d.2, stage (iii))]
2077	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}
2078	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorLinearMap}
2079	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2080	  With \(R\), \(A\), \(B\), \(x\) as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, the
2081	  additive forward map \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\) of
2082	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward} upgrades to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-\emph{linear}
2083	  map
2084	  \[
2085	    \mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap} \;\colon\;
2086	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\;
2087	      A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
2088	  \]
2089	  agreeing with \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\) on underlying additive maps, and hence
2090	  still characterised on germs of simple tensors by
2091	  \[
2092	    \mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr)
2093	      \;=\; \mathrm{germ}_x a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x b .
2094	  \]
2095	  This is the stage-(iii) landing of the construction narrated in
2096	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, and the direct input to stage (iv) (the reverse
2097	  map).

2099	
2100	The proof of the live whiskering lemma below factors through two named
2101	sub-lemmas: the \emph{d.1-bridge} relating membership in the sheafification
2102	localizer \(J.W\) to stalkwise isomorphism, and the \emph{second-factor
2103	naturality} of the d.2 commutation isomorphism \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2104	We state both before the lemma that consumes them.
2105	
2106	\begin{lemma}
2107	
2108	\leanok
2109	[d.1-bridge: locally bijective implies stalkwise iso]
2110	  \label{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}
2111	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_stalkFunctor_map_of_W}
2112	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
2113	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on the
2114	  small site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), equipped with its canonical Grothendieck topology
2115	  \(J\) satisfying \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\). Let
2116	  \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules whose underlying
2117	  morphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves lies in \(J.W\). Then for every point
2118	  \(x \in X\) the induced \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map
2119	  \[
2120	    (\mathtt{stalkFunctor}\,\mathtt{Ab}\,x).\mathtt{map}\,
2121	      \bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g\bigr)
2122	    \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
2123	  \]
2124	  is an isomorphism. Conversely, if the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism at
2125	  every point, then \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g \in J.W\).
2126	\end{lemma}
2127	
2128	\begin{proof}
2129	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
2130	  \leanok
2131	  Membership in \(J.W = J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\) unfolds to
2132	  local bijectivity: \(g\) is both \(J\)-locally injective and \(J\)-locally
2133	  surjective. On the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) the Grothendieck-topology
2134	  notions of local injectivity and local surjectivity coincide with the classical
2135	  ``locally on a cover'' conditions, and these are detected stalkwise: a morphism of
2136	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves is locally injective iff every stalk map is
2137	  injective and locally surjective iff every stalk map is surjective. Concretely, for
2138	  the topological site one identifies local bijectivity of
2139	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g\) with the associated sheaf map being an
2140	  isomorphism and applies the stalkwise criterion
2141	  \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\); equivalently one
2142	  combines \(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\) with the injective
2143	  stalk criterion \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.app\_injective\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_injective}\)
2144	  and the matching locally-surjective-on-stalks characterisation. A stalk map that is
2145	  both injective and surjective is an isomorphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalks, which is
2146	  the forward implication; the converse runs the same equivalences in reverse, since a
2147	  family of stalk isomorphisms forces local bijectivity. (The \(R_x\)-linear refinement
2148	  of these stalk maps, used downstream, is \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}.)
2149	\end{proof}

2151	\begin{lemma}
2152	% NOTE: iter-238 (review agent) — dangling `\lean{}` pin REMOVED. This B-naturality argument
2153	% was never formalized as a standalone declaration: the prover (iter-237) inlined it as the
2154	% `have key` step inside `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` (Vestigial.lean), to avoid
2155	% re-plumbing the +/0 carrier-duality at the module-morphism boundary twice. No decl named

2157	% is retained (unformalized) as an auxiliary statement because its `\label` is still consumed by
2158	% `\uses`/`\cref` downstream; its mathematical content is discharged inside the whiskering lemma.
2159	[B-naturality of the stalk--tensor comparison]
2160	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}
2161	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2162	  Let \(R\), \(A\) and \(x \in X\) be as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, and let
2163	  \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. The isomorphism
2164	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} is natural in the
2165	  \emph{second} tensor factor: the square
2166	  \[
2167	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2168	      (A \otimes^{p} M).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
2169	        & \xrightarrow{\ (A \triangleleft g)_x\ }
2170	        & (A \otimes^{p} N).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \\[4pt]
2171	      \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,M}
2172	        & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,N} \\[4pt]
2173	      A_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x
2174	        & \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id}_{A_x} \otimes\, g_x\ }
2175	        & A_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x
2176	    \end{array}
2177	  \]
2178	  commutes, where \(g_x\) is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk map of
2179	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} and \(\mathrm{id}_{A_x} \otimes\, g_x =
2180	  \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,A_x\,g_x\). Equivalently, under \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)
2181	  the stalk of the left-whiskered morphism \(A \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_A \otimes g\)
2182	  is identified with \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,A_x\,(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\,g\,x)\).
2183	\end{lemma}
2184	
2185	\begin{proof}
2186	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2187	  Both composites in the square are \(R_x\)-linear maps out of
2188	  \((A \otimes^{p} M).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\), so by \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\)
2189	  together with the joint epimorphy of the germ maps \(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\) it
2190	  suffices to check that they agree on germs of simple tensors
2191	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\), with \(a \in A(U)\) and \(m \in M(U)\). On such a
2192	  generator the left-then-bottom composite sends \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\) first
2193	  to \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x m\) by the germ characterisation
2194	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap\_germ\_tmul}\) of \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,M\)
2195	  (stage (iii) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}), and then to
2196	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x m)\) by definition of
2197	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\). The germ--functoriality characterisation
2198	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} gives
2199	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x m) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\), so this equals
2200	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\). The top-then-right composite
2201	  sends \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\) first to
2202	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x\bigl((A \triangleleft g)_U(a \otimes m)\bigr) =
2203	  \mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes g_U\,m)\) --- the whiskered map acts as
2204	  \(\mathrm{id}_A \otimes g\) sectionwise --- and then, again by
2205	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap\_germ\_tmul}\) for \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,N\),
2206	  to \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\). The two results agree, and
2207	  the inverse direction (the analogous identity for the reverse map
2208	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorRev}\) via \(\mathtt{stalkTensorRev\_germ\_tmul}\)) is checked the
2209	  same way. Hence the square commutes.
2210	\end{proof}

2214	\leanok
2215	[Unconditional left-whiskering of the localizer, via d.2]
2216	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
2217	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
2218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2219	  Let \(R\), \(J\) be as above with \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\),
2220	  let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\)
2221	  be a morphism whose underlying morphism lies in \(J.W\). Then the left-whiskered
2222	  morphism \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\) is \(J\)-locally injective
2223	  (indeed lies in \(J.W\)). No flatness and no local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) is
2224	  required. This discharges the open left-whiskering obligation
2225	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} that the unconditional associator
2226	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes.
2227	\end{lemma}
2228	
2229	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (Archon-original assembly from the Stacks stalk-tensor lemma
2230	% above and the stalkwise criterion for membership in the sheafification localizer;
2231	% no single external source proof.)
2232	\begin{proof}
2233	  \leanok
2234	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map,
2235	    lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}
2236	  The proof runs in three movements: the d.1-bridge turns membership in \(J.W\) into a
2237	  stalkwise statement, the second-factor naturality of the d.2 isomorphism transports
2238	  the whiskered stalk into a tensor of the stalk maps, and a flatness-free
2239	  ``tensoring by an equivalence'' argument concludes.
2240	
2241	  \emph{(a) The d.1-bridge.} Specialise to the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
2242	  where stalks exist. By the hypothesis \(g \in J.W\) and the d.1-bridge
2243	  \cref{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}, the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map \(g_x : M_x \to N_x\)
2244	  is an isomorphism at every point \(x \in X\). By \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} this
2245	  stalk map carries an \(R_x\)-linear structure, and the bijectivity just obtained
2246	  upgrades it to an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2247	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso} : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\).
2248	
2249	  \emph{(b) Identifying the whiskered stalk.} By the second-factor naturality of the
2250	  stalk--tensor comparison \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}, the
2251	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
2252	  identifies the stalk \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) of the whiskered morphism with the
2253	  map
2254	  \[
2255	    \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x \;=\; \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x
2256	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \longrightarrow F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
2257	  \]
2258	
2259	  \emph{(c) Conclusion.} Tensoring the \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2260	  \(g_x : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) of (a) on the left by the identity of \(F_x\)
2261	  yields an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2262	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\)
2263	  (this is \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.lTensor}\); it needs \emph{no} flatness of \(F_x\),
2264	  because lifting an isomorphism through \(F_x \otimes_{R_x} (-)\) only uses the
2265	  functoriality of the tensor product, and the inverse is supplied by the inverse
2266	  equivalence). By (b), \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is therefore an isomorphism of
2267	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalks for every \(x\). Applying the converse half of the d.1-bridge
2268	  \cref{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso} to the morphism \(F \triangleleft g\) gives
2269	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\), and in particular it is \(J\)-locally injective
2270	  (\(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\)). No flatness, no local
2271	  triviality, and the whiskering object \(F\) is arbitrary.
2272	
2273	  The statement holds over any Grothendieck site \(J\) satisfying
2274	  \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\), and is applied in particular
2275	  at the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) of a scheme, where the stalk
2276	  arguments of (a)--(c) are available and through which the unconditional associator
2277	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized.
2278	\end{proof}

2280	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
2281	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
2282	
2283	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
2284	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
2285	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2286	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
2287	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
2288	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
2289	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
2290	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
2291	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
2292	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
2293	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
2294	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
2295	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
2296	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

2416	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit

2419	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2420	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
2421	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
2422	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
2423	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
2424	  01CR).
2425	
2426	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
2427	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
2428	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
2429	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
2430	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2431	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
2432	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
2433	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2434	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
2435	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
2436	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
2437	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
2438	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
2439	
2440	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
2441	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
2442	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
2443	  \[
2444	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
2445	    \quad
2446	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
2447	    \quad
2448	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
2449	    \quad
2450	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
2451	  \]
2452	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
2453	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2454	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
2455	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
2456	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
2457	  any of the four axiom proofs.
2458	
2459	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
2460	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
2461	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
2462	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
2463	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
2464	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
2465	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
2466	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
2467	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
2468	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
2469	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
2470	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
2471	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
2472	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
2473	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
2474	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
2475	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
2476	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
2477	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
2478	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
2479	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
2480	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
2481	  pinned commit.
2482	
2483	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
2484	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
2485	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
2486	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
2487	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
2488	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
2489	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The associator

2515	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2516	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2517	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2518	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2519	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2520	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2521	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2522	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2523	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
2524	  \[
2525	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2526	    \quad
2527	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2528	    \quad
2529	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2530	    \quad
2531	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2532	  \]

2534	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2535	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2536	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).

2560	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2561	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback \emph{is}
2587	``extension of scalars'', and extension of scalars along any ring map \emph{is} strong
2588	monoidal --- the base-change tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong
2589	(M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) is an isomorphism with no flatness
2590	hypothesis. So at the level of mathematics there is no obstacle: pullback commutes
2591	with \(\otimes\) for every morphism. The one genuine subtlety is purely structural to
2592	the formalisation: the project carries \(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
2593	\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), so that \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\) has
2594	no sectionwise value to which one could literally attach the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2595	tensorator. The resolution is the standard one for an abstract left adjoint: build the
2596	strong-monoidal structure on a \emph{concrete} model \(P\) of the pullback and transport
2597	it to \(f^*\) along the uniqueness of left adjoints. This dissolves the
2598	``no sectionwise formula'' worry entirely --- one never computes \(f^*\) sectionwise;
2599	one transports a concrete monoidal structure across an adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
2600	This is exactly the device that closed \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
2601	
2602	It is worth recording why two tempting shortcuts are not taken. First, one might hope
2603	that since pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are
2604	preserved for free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal,
2605	yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
2606	oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
2607	comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. Second, one might route through local triviality,
2608	``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to
2609	locally \(\mathcal{O}\)''. This is a mirage on the present carrier: recovering
2610	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse bridge
Let me now continue reading the pullback monoidality section to find `lem:pullback_tensor_iso`, `lem:pullback_unit_iso`, and `lem:isinvertible_pullback`.

2610	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse bridge
2611	\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly the
2612	dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
2613	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. Restricting to \emph{flat}
2614	structure maps does not help either: flatness governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not
2615	whether the tensor comparison is an isomorphism (it is an isomorphism for \emph{every}
2616	morphism, flat or not). The strong-monoidal pullback route is therefore primary
2617	precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse witness directly, through a
2618	comparison isomorphism valid for all morphisms, never reintroducing the deleted dual.
2619	
2620	\begin{lemma}
2621	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2622	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2624	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2625	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2626	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2627	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2628	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2629	  \[
2630	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2631	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2632	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2633	  \]
2634	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2635	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2636	  and all morphisms \(f\).

2638	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2639	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2640	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).

2645	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
2646	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
2647	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})

2651	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2652	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2653	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2654	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2655	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
2656	\end{lemma}
2657	
2658	\begin{proof}
2659	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2660	  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
2661	  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
2662	  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
2663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
2664	  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
2665	  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
2666	  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
2667	  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
2668	  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
2669	  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
2670	  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
2671	  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
2672	  argument has three steps.
2673	
2674	  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
2675	  \[
2676	    P \;=\; \mathtt{sheafification} \;\circ\; (\text{presheaf base change}),
2677	  \]
2678	  the presheaf-level inverse image (sectionwise extension of scalars along the
2679	  structure-sheaf ring maps of \(f\)) followed by sheafification on the small Zariski
2680	  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
2681	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
2682	  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
2683	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
2684	  \[
2685	    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
2686	      \;\cong\;
2687	    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
2688	  \]
2689	  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
2690	  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2691	  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
2692	  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
2693	  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).
2694	
2695	  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
2696	  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
2697	  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
2698	  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,
2699	  so the composite \(P\) is left adjoint to the pushforward.
2700	
2701	  \emph{Step 3 (transport to the abstract pullback).} By definition
2702	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
2703	  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward. By the uniqueness of left adjoints
2704	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) --- the same device that closed
2705	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- there is a canonical natural isomorphism
2706	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f \cong P\). The strong-monoidal structure of
2707	  \(P\) travels along this isomorphism, so its tensorator becomes a genuine
2708	  \emph{isomorphism}
2709	  \[
2710	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2711	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2712	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
2713	  \]
2714	  natural in \(M\) and \(N\). This is the asserted comparison; the abstract pullback is
2715	  never evaluated sectionwise --- the monoidal structure is transported across the
2716	  adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
2717	
2718	  Two shortcuts are deliberately avoided. The bundled doctrinal adjunction
2719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) is present at the pinned commit, but
2720	  it is not usable here: it requires \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{SheafOfModules})\)
2721	  instances (the project carries \(\otimes_X\) as a bespoke sheafified tensor with no
2722	  such instance and no \(\mathtt{pushforward.LaxMonoidal}\)), and in any case it would
2723	  yield only an \emph{oplax} comparison \emph{map}, not the required isomorphism. And a
2724	  mere oplax map does not suffice: an (op)lax monoidal functor does \emph{not} preserve
2725	  invertible objects for free --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet
2726	  \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so proving the
2727	  comparison is an isomorphism, as Step~3 does, is genuinely necessary.
2728	
2729	  The full strong-monoidal packaging of \(f^*\) is delivered by the transported
2730	  structure \(P.\mathtt{Monoidal}\); the invertibility consumer
2731	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} consumes only the comparison \emph{isomorphism} at
2732	  its witness pair, which Step~3 supplies.
2733	\end{proof}

2739	  \label{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
2740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
2741	  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write

2753	  \]
2754	  where \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)_* \cong f_* \circ h_*\) is the

2756	  but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the
2757	  nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit

2763	  Both sides are morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it suffices to compare them on
2764	  sections over each open. Each comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\) acts on sections as the
2765	  structure-sheaf ring homomorphism induced by the relevant morphism of schemes, and the
2766	  pseudofunctor coherence \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) acts as the identification of
2767	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)\)-pushforward sections with iterated \(f_*\,h_*\)-pushforward
2768	  sections. The composition coherence is therefore the functoriality identity
2769	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ f^\sharp\) of the structure-sheaf ring maps,
2770	  which holds definitionally; the two sides agree section by section.
2771	\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
$f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
= f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
\end{lemma}
```
